quality.rationale: 完整洛伦兹生成元微分算符推导+对易关系严格证明与泡利矩阵映射+有限变换指数化构造

origin.question: Problem 1.25. Let $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}}$ be infinitesimal rotation operators defined so that $1 + \mathrm{i}\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{j}}\theta / 2$ is a rotation by a small angle $\theta$ around the j-axis. Let $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{z}}$ be infinitesimal boost operators defined so that $1 + \mathrm{i}\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{j}}\mathbf{v} / 2$ is a boost by a small velocity $\mathbf{v}$ in the j-direction. Show that the following relationships, and all their cyclic permutations, are true:$$\begin{array}{l}[ J_{x}, J_{y} ] = 2 i J_{z} \\[ J_{x}, K_{y} ] = 2 i K_{z} \\\left[ \mathrm {K}_{\mathbf {x}}, \mathrm {K}_{\mathbf {y}}\right] = - 2 \mathrm {i} \mathrm {J}_{z}. \\\end{array}$$Find a representation of the Lorentz group in terms of Pauli spin matrices $\sigma_{\mathbf{x}}$ , $\sigma_{\mathbf{y}}$ , $\sigma_{\mathbf{z}}$ , and the unit matrix.
origin.answer: $\mathbf{J} \to \underline{\sigma}$, $\mathbf{K} \to i\underline{\sigma}$, finite transformation $L(\underline{\theta}, \underline{v}) = \cos h q + (\underline{q} \cdot \underline{\sigma} / q) \sin h q$ with $\underline{q} = (i\underline{\theta}^* - \underline{v}^*)/2$.
origin.solution: Solution 1.25. Let us first derive the forms of the infinitesimal operators by considering their action on a spacetime function $f(x, y, z, t)$ :(i) Rotations about $\mathbf{z}$ axis:$$\begin{array}{l}\mathbf {x}^{\prime} \approx \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {y} \theta \\\mathbf {y}^{\prime} \approx \mathbf {y} + \mathbf {x} \theta \\z^{\prime} = z \\t^{\prime} = t, \\\end{array}$$$$f \left(x^{\prime}, y^{\prime}, z^{\prime}\right) - f (x, y, z) = \theta \left[ - y \partial_{x} + x \partial_{y}\right] f , $$thus$$J_{z} = - 2 \mathrm {i} \left(\mathrm {x} \partial_{\mathrm {y}} - \mathrm {y} \partial_{\mathrm {x}}\right), $$since$$\mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x}^{\prime}, \mathbf {y}^{\prime}, z^{\prime}) = (1 + \mathrm {i} \mathbf {J}_{z} \theta / 2) \mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y}, z).$$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{y}}$ $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}$ are obtained by cyclical permutations of Equation (3).(ii) Boosts in $\mathbf{z}$ direction:$$\begin{array}{l}z^{\prime} \approx z - v t \\t^{\prime} \approx t - v x \\\mathbf {y}^{\prime} = \mathbf {y} \\z^{\prime} = z \\\end{array}$$$$f (x^{\prime}, y^{\prime}, z^{\prime}, t^{\prime}) - f (x, y, z, t) = \left[ - t \partial_{z} - z \partial_{t}\right] v f (x, y, z, t)$$thus$$\mathrm {K}_{z} = 2 \mathrm {i} \left(\mathrm {t} \frac {\partial}{\partial z} + z \frac {\partial}{\partial t}\right), \text {e t c .}$$(iii) Now, commutation relations may be worked out from Equations (3) and (4) by the usual procedure, for example:$$\left[ J_{x}, J_{y}\right] = - 4 \left[ y \partial_{z} - z \partial_{y}, z \partial_{x} - x \partial_{z}\right] = 4 \left(x \partial_{y} - y \partial_{x}\right) = 2 i J_{z}, $$and similarly for all other commutators.(iv) A representation of the Lorentz group is specified by giving explicit matrices for the generators. We can associate the operators with the Pauli spin matrices$$\sigma_{\mathbf {x}} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c}0 & 1 \\\mathbf {1} & 0\end{array}\right] \quad \text {e t c .}$$according to$$\begin{array}{l}\underline {{\mathbf {J}}}\rightarrow \underline {{\sigma}} \\\underline {{\mathbf {K}}}\rightarrow \underline {{\mathrm {i} \sigma}}. \\\end{array}$$The commutation relations for $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ and $\underline{\mathbf{K}}$ are satisfied since $[\sigma_{\mathbf{x}},\sigma_{\mathbf{y}}] = 2\mathrm{i}\sigma_{z}$ etc. To find the matrices corresponding to finite transformations $\mathbf{L}$ , note that for example $\mathbf{J}_{z}$ is defined by$$\left. \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {L}}{\mathrm {d} \theta}\right|_{\theta = 0} = \frac {\mathrm {i} \mathbf {J}_{z}}{2} ,$$so that$$\mathrm {L} (\theta) = \exp \left(\mathrm {i} \theta \mathrm {J}_{z} / 2\right).$$An arbitrary finite transformation $\mathbf{L}$ will have six parameters; in the general case the rotation parameters $\underline{\theta}^{*}$ (here treated as a 3-vector) will be different from the physical rotation angle $\underline{\theta}$ and the parameters representing boosts, $\underline{\mathbf{v}}^{*}$ , will differ from the physical relative velocity $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ . These are details; the Lorentz group is defined completely by its generators $\underline{\mathbf{J}}, \underline{\mathbf{K}}$ ; its parametrization is largely arbitrary. The infinitesimal version of an arbitrary transformation $\mathbf{L}$ is$$\delta \mathrm {L} = \mathrm {i} \left(\theta_{\mathrm {x}}^{*} \mathrm {J}_{\mathrm {x}} + \theta_{\mathrm {y}}^{*} \mathrm {J}_{\mathrm {y}} + \theta_{\mathrm {z}}^{*} \mathrm {J}_{\mathrm {z}} + \mathrm {v}_{\mathrm {x}}^{*} \mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {x}} + \mathrm {v}_{\mathrm {y}}^{*} \mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {y}} + \mathrm {v}_{\mathrm {z}}^{*} \mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {z}}\right) / 2 = (\mathrm {i} \underline {{\theta}}^{*} - \underline {{\mathrm {v}}}^{*}) \cdot \underline {{\sigma}} / 2.$$Let us define$$\underline {{\mathbf {q}}} \equiv (\underline {{\mathbf {i} \theta}}^{*} - \underline {\mathbf {v}}^{*}) / 2$$$$q^{2} \equiv \underset {\sim} {q} \cdot \underset {\sim} {q} = \frac {1}{4} \left(- \theta^{* 2} - 2 i \underset {\sim} {\theta}^{*} \cdot v^{*} + v^{2}\right).$$(Note that pure boosts and pure rotations correspond respectively to pure real and pure imaginary $\underline{\mathbf{q}}$ .) We have then, for the general finite transformation:$$\begin{array}{l}L (\underline {{\theta}}, \underline {v}) = \exp (\underline {{q}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}}) = \sum_{n = 0}^{\inf ty} (\underline {{q}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}})^{n} / n! \\= \sum_{n = 0, 2, 4 \dot s}^{\inf ty} q^{n} / n! + \sum_{n = 1, 3, 5 \dot s}^{\inf ty} (\underline {{q}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}}) q^{n - 1} / n! \\= \cos h q + (\underline {{q}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}} / q) \sin h q. \\\end{array}$$Here we have used$$(\underset {\sim} {q} \cdot \underset {\sim} {\sigma}) (\underset {\sim} {q} \cdot \underset {\sim} {\sigma}) = q_{i} q_{j} (\sigma_{i}, \sigma_{j}) = q_{i} q_{j} \delta^{i j} = q^{2}.$$
